speaker: interviewer
text: It's November 13, 1995. I'm interviewing a righteous Gentile called Irene Gut-Opdyke. Her maiden name is Gutova. My name is Renee Firestone. We are in Yorba Linda, California, in the United States. And the language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 19-- November 13, 1995. I'm interviewing a righteous Gentile, Irene Gut-Opdyke. Her maiden name is Gutova. I am Renee Firestone. We are in Yorba Linda, California, in the United States. And the language will be English. Good morning, Irene. Thank you so much for inviting us to give your testimony to us today. To start with, I would like you to tell me your name and spell it for me, and tell me where and when were you born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland. My name was Irene-- 'Ee-renn-uh'-- Irene Gut. And, you know, in Poland, you say, Mr. Gut, Mrs. Gutova, or Miss Gutovna. I was not married, young girl. And I did have four younger sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, please tell me your present name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My present name is Opdyke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-P-D-Y-K-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gutovna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-T-O-W--

speaker: interviewer
text: G-U-- is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gu-- G-U-T-O-W. You know, I have a hard time to spell it without looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what town were you born in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in little town of Kozienice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only-- Kozienice, I think, is by Kielce or Radom or something like that. I don't really know. I know that was I was one year old when we moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were very young. My father did go to the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was one year  old. I don't know. We moved so many times. So I don't know from that. I think we moved to Poznan or someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Wladislaw Gut. And he was an architect. And he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] building a factory. And also, he was a chemist. He did do the thing for the electric-- electric poles. He was only one. He did have even connection with that with friends in Germany, in other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Our father was a wonderful man. I don't remember him ever spank us. But he was stern. And my mother was a saint.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maria, from [INAUDIBLE]. My mother was a disciplinarian. She did teach us. Even-- even we did have a maid, but she didn't allow her to clean our room. We did have to learn to-- to do. She says, you can marry a millionaire. But you have to know how to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many siblings? How many brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only four young sisters, younger sisters. That's the reason, really, my mother took care of us. And we were brought up in the Catholic faith. But my mother really taught us the Ten Commandments. We did have to know how to do. And we have to understand that we not alone in the world, that we have-- we have to be good to people and help people. When we were coming from schools, we brought the dogs and birds and all kinds of animals. My mother never said, this is not-- not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your sisters' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters' names-- Janina, Maria, Bronislawa, and Wladislawa. They are wonderful girls. I spent with them very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much younger were they than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the youngest one is about-- around 10 years younger. You know, we were really very close together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I finished-- I was in Radom, then in [INAUDIBLE] in school because my father was moving. And the last high school, that was in Aberschlidien, close to German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Koswawa ?] [? Gora. ?] And I did belong to the group-- Polish young girls dancing group, singing group, theatrical group. And most of all, I wanted be a nurse. So I was with the group young women that went to the hospital-- candy striper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's go back for a moment. When you were going to elementary school, were you bringing friends home to your house? Yes. Yes. We were bringing-- that was in Radom, I remember. And I remember even that there was a wonderful young Jewish family. And the little girl was very sick. And so the parents were very happy to have me to come and speak to her and play with her so she would not feel so lonely. Was she your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was older. She was younger, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school, in your class, did you have any Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember because that was not the point in my life. My parents taught me-- we [? didn't ?] have many Jewish friends. So my parents taught us that there is no difference and that we are all the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents associate with Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes, even in Radom, when my father worked with Mr. [? Hellmann. ?] He was-- my father worked for him, for Mr. [? Hellmann. ?] I know I was at that time maybe in the oh, grade school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Mr. [? Hellmann ?] do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did have factory. He ran a factory when my father was with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, associating--

speaker: interviewer
text: Associating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the time. I met-- I can tell-- tell you truly I did not know the word of 'anti-Semitism,' my sisters also. We did not know. I learned that during the war and after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that there is such a thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: As anti-Semitism? No, I did not know the word 'anti-Semitism.' I know that we were all-- all friends. There was no difference. So I cannot tell you that, really. I cannot tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What years did you go to high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well, I was finishing high school-- it was 1938. 1938, I was finishing high school with the greatest desire to be another Florence Nightingale. I dreamed to go to a far country to bring help. And being a candy striper in the hospital where the nuns were, you know, I was very impressed with the beautiful white hat. They were so gorgeous. They were so good, like angels. I told my father, that's what I wanted to be. He said, fine, but first finish-- finish good nursing school. And if you want it, will be OK with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you go to the nursing school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1938, my father signed me to the nursing school. But it was 200 kilometers from my home. We lived six kilometers from German border. And the school was in Radom, in middle of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in beginning of 1939, actually, I sign-- I sign in. And I was in Radom. I lived in-- with other girls that were studying. And we were supposed to study in school, but also work in a hospital, practice. Let's go back again. You-- you told me that your parents and your family was Catholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother taught us to be good and to behave. And we did go. I could not say that we were going every Sunday, really, to church. You know? Then, when I was in high school, we did go. I-- I was in the Catholic school. So with the school, we did go to church every Sunday. But telling the truth, I myself, If I needed really to talk to God, and I could not concentrate in school because the young girls, you know, pushing, laughing-- and away from school, there was a beautiful, beautiful forest. And that was my God. I could kneel down or sit by the beautiful tree and speak to God. And I felt that he's there. He is there. I could speak. I could feel him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything in the teaching of the church that-- that you didn't like or that you disagreed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not that I didn't like. I could not understand-- to me, God was like good father. I could go to him tell him everything, and he's forgiving. He's loving. And sometimes the priest painted God in such a stern way that I felt if I would drop a piece of bread on the-- on the floor, I go straight to-- to hell. And I could not-- I could not agree with that. I mean, that's the reason I felt if I needed prayer, praying in a-- in a forest by the trees, in the beauty of the nature, that I felt-- I felt very warm. And I felt I could say it, everything. And I would be heard, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went to learn to be a nurse amongst the nuns, did you become more religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were nuns in that hospital in Radom, but there were students, not-- they were students like me, girls. And there were also older nurses, finished nurses, that were also not nuns. And first, I was just interested to learn. I wanted to be the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with the girls, I didn't go to movies. I didn't want anything. I wanted my parents to be proud. I did not-- at that moment, I did not think that I will be a-- I wanted to study first. And before we really started, Hitler was there. Were there any non-Jewish women in this nursing school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. See, to me, this would not have made it any difference. You know, I didn't make it any particular-- I don't-- I don't know if there were, I cannot tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any close friends with anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, because many of the girls were going with boys and interested-- and I-- my interest was not such.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was 17, going on 18, you know? And my interest was not really with-- with boys. I was, as a youngster, I did have the beginning of tuberculosis. I was developing rather late. And I was not interested. I thought I am ugly. I thought myself-- my sister, Janina, that was one year younger, she was developing little breasts. And I'm not. So I did not like myself. So I will not tell you that-- I did not have any boys looking at me or I looking for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in this-- did you live in this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sisters? Where were they at this--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were with my parents. You know, and just before [INAUDIBLE] we start to hearing that the-- 1938-- that the Germans were-- wanted to go fight with Poland. But I was 200 kilometers. And like everything, we did not believe it, you know? One day I was going to school through the empty field. There were ditches dug always-- the military. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I heard overhead the heavy roar of airplanes. And I looked. And I did see something falling from them. And then explosions, fires. I was so bewildered.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody pulled me into the ditch. That was one of the doctors from the hospital. He said, dummy, what you staying and watching? The war, the war is there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when the airplanes left, we went to the hospital. And it was one big nightmare. There were so many people already bought in that were wounded-- and people looking for each other. On the way going, there were horses that were torn apart. It was unbelievable. And we just decided to help. We worked feverishly day and night trying to help the wounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 19-- that was September 1, 1939. So that was maybe, at that time, September 3 or 4. And I remember that they said that the Germans are moving towards middle-- they took my place with my parents right away. But they were moving towards Radom. And some of the nurses, the oldest nurses, they took-- they joined Polish Army. And they took me with them because I didn't know what I would do without. So they took me with them. And for days, we were on the run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before this happened, did you know the name Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now I-- I know that Hitler is moving. Not before-- 1938 was something said that Hitler is-- but I-- I was-- heard that already in Radom when I was-- that Hitler is coming to fight. But I did not know that he was fight-- the Kristallnacht, or-- I did not know that. But I knew that he was-- he is-- he is moving on-- on Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jews from Germany coming to Poland before '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. See, I was so busy trying to study, to learn. I will not tell you that I was-- know about that. I know that my father did work with Mr. [? Hellman. ?] Then in-- in [? Koswawa ?] [? Gora ?] there was another Mr. Gostchorzewicz. He was also Jewish. And we were very good-- my parents were very good friends with them. And you know, in Poland, little girls were not taught politic. I was taught to be good mother-- wife and mother. That's all. Today the children are learning politics. But I did not. I-- I-- I did not know about politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were running with the nurses--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were retreating.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did the nuns go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The nuns stayed with the hospital. But the nurse-- the students and the nurses that were not nuns, we were going with the Polish Army. And every five minutes, the Germans were coming and bombarding, coming over their heads. People were running, escaping. They say, OK, if we only leave the Vistula pass, you know, and we will be able to stop some. Well, we stopped at the-- with the little-- with the big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Vistula?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vistula is the-- is the river, you know, the Polish river. And-- and we would say that it will take a little time the German to pass us. So we were in some hospital trying to help the wounded because they were in the Red Cross. You know that they even were bombarding. They did see the Red Cross, but that did not make any difference. So we were trying to change the wound-- wounds and things. And of a second, they-- about 3 o'clock in the morning, they say, hey, the Germans are crossing the river. They have-- what do they call it-- [? tampoons, ?] something, you know, blew up. And they were crossing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in that hospital there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was sometime just past the Vistula. I-- I don't remember. We were so tired. And we came in the evening. And then 4 o'clock in the morning, we were supposed to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get there, on foot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were with the military cars. You know, yeah. And when they say-- so we were packing the wounded on a-- on a-- two cars. And the cars were taking them to the railroad station, packing them there. I remember I was going with the last transport. And when we-- when we were coming to the railroad station, there was attack. The Germans were coming and bombarding. And the train left. And there were about a locomotive and another three or four-- four cars. Because they threw burning bombs. And there were big holes in the walls of the train. Where-- when they left, we put everybody into the trains. And-- but the-- the trucks were broken. It took the men, some-- the Polish men, military, whoever it was. They were trying to straighten out the-- and-- so the train can leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have wounded people in the train. There was no doctor because that left. I remember going from one car to another and asking, is there a doctor? Is there a doctor? It took us a long time to get almost to the Russian border. So that-- Polish military was retreating, and Hitler was pursuing. So where-- where did you wind up?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was close to Lvov, some-- near there. And at that time, there was many parts of Polish Army, they were trying to pick up strength and fight the Germans. We were just coming-- we were the last train that comes in. I remember that we were called to meeting, everybody. And the Polish general said, thank you so much. The war is, for us, over. Hitler and Stalin took my country. Poland is no more. You are free to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain to us what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the Polish general-- because all army were retreating. And Hitler was pursuing. And so they tried to fight.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before we ever to do so, Stalin and Hitler made a pact. They-- they said, OK, we take Poland. And so the Polish general said, thank you, with tears in his eyes. Polish officers were taking their insignia. And what we-- what we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barn houses were open. So I remember we did have a like a little truck car, part of the Polish Army, maybe 40 soldiers-- officers-- and about eight nurses. We packed everything-- the staples and blankets and everything. And we escape to the forest, big Ukrainian forest there. And-- but it's September, end of September. Then the next month coming-- was already cold in Poland. We went not prepared to really live underneath the trees.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from the blankets, we made a place underneath the trees. And we did have staples, but we did not have food, no. So one day, four of the men, another woman, and I, we went to villages, Ukrainian villages, trying to barter. I was the youngest. And they left me on a watch. And they were going between the houses trying to barter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of  a second-- the night was so bright; there was snow on the ground. And I heard noise. When I looked at it, the big Russian truck was coming. And the Russian soldiers were jumping out with the bayonets affixed. I was so scared. I was so scared, so I ran. I ran to the forest because that was my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where this was, where was this split where the Russians and the Germans met?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that it was in the end of three week-- September 1, the war started. And that was towards the end of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the location where-- where the borders were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They say, it's-- half-- half of the-- half of the side was taken by Russian and half was taken by Germans. I mean, that's-- that's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wound up, at this point, on the Russian side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
